Something just popped out to me today and it's something that I really haven't spoken about in a while. That is, you know, the importance of maintaining rapport. That rapport is not a constant. You know, I've touched on this over the last couple months but it's really reiterated you, like the importance of not only just getting into rapport but being keenly or acutely aware that you cannot move forward on the straight line. You can't move forward in the sale if you're not in rapport. And what happens is based on the last words that you said, less tonality you used, it connected with the person, the prospect. You end up having a slight uptick in rapport and if what you said kind of didn't connect with the pros and the pistol off in some way, you have this down-ticking rapport. And by the way, sometimes you say something, one thing and it just totally blows rapport and you fall out of rapport. And if you continue to try to move down the straight line way and not in rapport, you're done. You can't close anybody. So just remember, what are the reasons why you're, what I realize more than anything, more and more, as I just keep getting so entrenched and teaching the straight line all over the world is that every aspect of the straight line is interconnected. That's why it works so well. And also the in-game aspects are connected as well. So in other words, if you break rapport, right, what happens is you can transfer certainty because people tune out. And once you break rapport, right, the second 10, you, they trust and get you, that's done. So you can't transfer any certainty to the other person. So what you need to do is you need to stop and you need to back up and get back into rapport. That means not, okay, let me slow down. In other words, as you move down the straight line, right, what you're doing is you're slowly but surely step by step creating this air tight logical scream from the Hiltop's logical case and also creating an airtight emotional case. That's what you're doing, right? For all three 10s that eventually lower in the action threshold. And if necessary, adding on pain, the pain that you actually, you know, extracted or pulled out during the qualifying session, the intelligence guy, right? So that's what's really going on. But if you say something that pisses the prospect of it doesn't connect with them and you sense that slippage in rapport, right? If you keep trying to build certainty and keep making your case, you just keep falling. It's like this death spiral where you fall deeper and deeper and deeper out of rapport until basically you're done at a certain point. You just have no shot. You're blown out. And I've seen this all the time. Like here's a perfect example where someone will be making a presentation salesperson, right? And I'm listening to them training the sales force, right? And they'll say something about the prospect is great. I was doing this with some of the trading system. And the guy asked the woman a question. He was like, so yeah, this thing is just, it's so great. It worked so well. And the woman's like, I don't really know. You know, she's like, I'm not really sure it's really that. You know, I don't know. I'm still skeptical. He's like, oh, it's just great. And he's kept going and saying, believe me, just trust me here's the great. And he kept trying to move down the line. And you know, the words don't really matter. The point was, is that they had this fundamental, he asked a question. And he said, does that make sense to you? And she's like, no. He's like, okay, well, anyway, he kept going. You can't do that. In the words, the key is, is that we have these stopping off points along the way for a reason. So when you, the reason the script is constructed with these stopping off points is to make sure that a, you don't just drone on and on because people tune out and you fall at our poor. And also to make sure that what you say, it logically connects with the other person because if it doesn't, you have to stop, loop back and remake your case again until you get, you say, does that make sense? And I said, oh, yeah, yeah, now I get it. If you, if you said something that didn't quite make sense to the person, it wasn't strong enough, maybe they just got confused or whatever, they just fundamentally disagree with you, you can't keep moving forward and try to close because all that happens is the person's like, like, you're not hearing me. You're not here. And they fall deeper and deeper. They close down. You fall more and more at our poor until you're done. The key is remember this rapport is not a constant. It goes up, it goes down. It's never the same. It's like when you watch one of these, an old country you live in, but they have these like, you know, when you see politicians debate, right? And they'll have like the studio audience and the studio ones, they all hold onto these buttons. And it's like, they press like, yes or no, agree or disagree, based on what the person is saying. And then you'll see like when the power, a politician is saying something that is actually true and it makes sense and it resonates with the audience, you'll see the green button, like the thought to rise at their inner, if they're in rapport, you update their, their, they agree with the politician. And then when they start to enter every lie or exaggerate, it goes, and then the report won't, and you see it like the hallway through the up and that's what happens as you move someone down the straight line from the open to the close. It's even with me, no matter how careful I am. Every time, like if you, when you ask someone to buy from you, to like sound, it's a dip. You must have enough money built up in your rapport bank, so to speak, that when you finally ask for the order, you ask someone to expend energy, right? As you're doing, you ask for the order to spend money, buy something, that's a negative. Free is a positive, right? So you ask someone to expend the energy, so it's a dip in the negative column. So what you've been trying to do through the entire presentation is you've built up so much equity in your win column that they say, yes, you know, based on the negative, how to spend money, the energy out versus the benefits in all the wonderful stuff I'm going to get when I say yes, you end up closing the sale. That's the calculation. That calculation also happens with rapport. So if you built up massive rapport with someone and then, you know, you say something that doesn't quite resonate, well, yeah, you'll have a dip, but you'll be okay. You'll dip that, or you want to raise it back up. Be careful about that. But the point is you can, you're, you get into this ultra tight rapport so that when you get towards the close and you ask for the order, they feel comfortable enough with you. They trust you enough that they'll put that best foot forward and they'll give you a shot. Addicts, essence, what the straight line basically is in terms of opening up an account, right? Opening up a new customer is also second sale, third sale, up sales, but for the opening trade, the opening sale, the opening purchase, right? The basis of the straight line is give me a shot to show you what I can do for you. Give me one percent of your trust. And again, don't worry about the cheap sale lines. It's not, whatever the line that is congruent for what you're selling is, it doesn't, but the point is it's about, I'm not looking to work big here. Give me a shot and believe me, you'll be glad you did. That's, that's what the street, there's a straight line is about like laying out this perfectly logical air type case about why there's absolutely no reason why they should say no. They can get easy for them to buy. Remember, energy in benefits out massive amount of benefits going out to them, only a little expenditure of energy in terms of money, how easy it is to get started. And that all that glue that runs on both sides of the line inside those boundaries is the rapport that you've created to your tonality and your body language, which is that unconscious communication, right? Establishing those two main core elements that devise rapport. They are number one, that you care. You care about them. You're not just there to make a sale. I care about you. I want to do the best thing for you. I'm not just in this to make money. Now remember, when you first meet someone, you can't say, you know, I really care about you, John or Jill. You say, what a fucking lying sack. You know, the word, you can't say that down the road. Yes, you can because you've proved it through your actions. But when you first meet someone as a salesman, say, do I really, really care about you? A lying salesman, right? The words don't exist in the beginning. So the way we get that point of course that we care, remember, it comes from your tonality, empathetic, sympathetic. I'm telling you, that's the magic. It's the rapport is not in the heart, what you do is the, uh-huh. Oh, wow. Oh, nice. The smile, the connection, the eye contact, the trust, it's unconscious communication, tonality, body language. That's the key. So that first element again is I care. I'm not just in this to make a buck like other salesmen. I care about you, I want to do the right thing by you. And once that happens, that paves the way for everything else, right? And the second aspect of rapport is called likability. I'm just like you and I like you. In other words, we associate with people based on our commonalities, not on differences. So like the old saying I always use is that you wouldn't say to your wife, oh, honey, guess what? I just went to a country club and I went in and walked around. There's not a soul there who's like me. You know, I'm a Republican, narrow Democrats. I like God. They all like tennis. We don't want the income. So I joined. You know what I'm saying? What the fuck is wrong with you? You're crazy, right? You would never do that. What you would do is say, I walked in there. Oh my God, they're just like me. Yeah, the same political parties, same religion, same interests. We all had a good few last couple. I joined. We associate. We join. We join forces based on having things in common. So the perception must be I'm just like you. Now, there's a subtlety to that. It's not about I'm just like you're like, oh, you like fishing. I like fishing. Not that. I don't mean that. I'm saying is that I speak at the same pace as you. I understand you. I feel your pain. Oh, yeah. Yeah. Right. And it's finding common humor. It's not about, you know, listen, if you sometimes just get lucky, is that, you know, you walk in and there's something that's obvious you have a common ally. Of course, you'd build on that. But don't play the game that whatever they like. If the fish is on the wall, oh my God, you're like, I like fishing. If it's as you see a picture of them scouting, you're like, I like skydiving. We'll fucking take your skydiving the next day. You'll be screwed, right? The point is, is that it's not that it's about listening to someone. Oh, yeah. I'm just like you. Yeah, I get it. I speak at the same speed. I listen to you. That's rapport. It's powerful stuff. And remember, along with that rapport comes that, you know, greatly misunderstood charisma, right? Which is basically that next level report. I master rapport, charisma, the ability to create rapport, some of the charisma, and it's a learnable skill, right? It's effective body language, appropriate tonality, and not saying stupid. So there are wrong words to say, right? That's the case of remember, don't ignore the fact that when you're actively selling, you're persuading, watch, look for cues that at any time that you're falling at a rapport, if you find that, slow down, back up, loop back, and get back in rapport by making sure they're on the same page with you. Usually, when rapport gets broken, it is because you say something and they're not on the same. They don't agree with you and you keep trying to move forward and plow through that. You're not listening to them. So listen. If there's some sort of discrepancy, they don't agree with you, you loop back, you make your case in a different way. And so you see what I'm saying now, say, oh, yeah, I get it. Exactly. And then you move forward.
